$comment: CONTRACT FIXTURE — backup file-mode exclusion globs (issue #2473).
This table is the single definition of the exclusion-glob dialect. It is replayed against BOTH sides:
If the two dialects ever drift apart, both suites go red. Do not edit one side's expectations to make a test pass.
The dialect is Go stdlib path.Match applied PER SEGMENT — NOT doublestar, NOT minimatch, NOT bash.
Cases tagged "divergence" are where a generic glob library would give a different answer; they are the
reason this could not be a one-line zod refinement.
validity[]  usable=true means the agent compiles and uses the pattern; usable=false means the agent
silently DROPS it (logs 'ignoring invalid exclusion pattern'). The API rejects exactly the
usable=false/problem=syntax set, and strips the problem=empty set.
matching[]  expected = does the agent's matcher exclude relPath given patterns?

validity.divergence: minimatch/bash treat '[!...]' as NEGATION. path.Match treats '!' as a LITERAL class member. Syntactically fine either way, so we MUST accept it — but it means something different. See matching[] for the proof.

Reversed range. path.Match does NOT error on hi<lo; it just never matches. Almost certainly a typo, but the agent accepts it, so an over-strict API would break a save that works today. Deliberately let through.

HEADLINE CASE. The trailing-dash-means-literal-dash idiom is valid in bash/minimatch/doublestar and is extremely common. Go's path.Match rejects it (getEsc errors on ']' after '-'). Today the agent silently drops this pattern and backs up everything the tech thought was excluded.
leading '-' in class: valid in bash, ErrBadPattern in Go
POSIX/bash allow ']' as the first class member ('[]]' matches ']'). Go rejects it.

validity.note: unclosed class in a path segment

validity.divergence: Valid as ONE glob string, but the agent matches slash patterns PER SEGMENT, so this splits into the malformed segments 'a[x' and 'y]b' and is dropped. A whole-string validator would wrongly accept it.

validity.note: empty interior segment can never match

validity.divergence: A trailing backslash is a TRAILING-ESCAPE syntax error in raw path.Match. But the agent folds '\'->'/' and trims separators BEFORE matching, so this is just the base-name pattern 'temp'. Validating the raw string with path.Match would wrongly reject it.

validity.note: Windows separators are folded to '/' — becomes the path pattern AppData/Local/Temp

validity.divergence: In path.Match/minimatch, '\*' is an ESCAPED literal star. Here the backslash is folded to '/' first, so this is the two-segment path pattern 'foo/*bar'. Backslash is NEVER an escape in this dialect.

validity.note: blank — agent skips; API strips rather than erroring

matching.note: base-name pattern matches at any depth, files AND dirs
base-name pattern matches a DIRECTORY named like the glob (walker then skips the subtree)
base-name pattern does not match a parent segment, only the base name
'*' does not cross a separator
'?' matches exactly one char
'?' does not match two chars

matching.divergence: IMPLICIT LEADING '**/'. A bare doublestar/minimatch Match('node_modules/**', 'app/node_modules/react/index.js') is FALSE — the pattern is anchored at the root. The agent prepends '**/' so exclusions are gitignore-style and match at ANY depth. This is the single biggest semantic divergence from a stock glob library.
'dir/**' matches the DIRECTORY ITSELF, not just its contents (doublestar historically required 'dir' or 'dir/**' separately). Matters: the walker needs the hit on the dir to skip the whole subtree.

matching.note: 'dir/**' matches a nested occurrence of the directory itself
leading doublestar spans any number of segments
trailing '**' spans ZERO segments — matches the dir itself
path pattern must still match every literal segment
multi-segment path pattern, non-matching middle segment

matching.divergence: '[!ab]' — path.Match reads '!' as a LITERAL class member, so this matches the file literally named '!x'. minimatch would read it as 'NOT a or b' and NOT match '!x'.
The same pattern under minimatch's negation semantics WOULD match 'cx' (c is not a or b). Under path.Match it does not — 'c' is not one of the literal class members {!, a, b}.

matching.note: '^' IS the negation character in path.Match
'^' negation excludes the listed members

matching.divergence: Reversed range never matches (but is NOT a syntax error — see validity[]).
Backslash is folded to '/', NOT treated as an escape: 'foo\*bar' behaves as the path pattern 'foo/*bar'.

matching.note: Windows-style pattern folds to a path pattern and matches at any depth
trailing backslash normalizes to a plain base-name pattern
case-SENSITIVE (linux/macos): pattern case must match
case-INSENSITIVE (windows): pattern case is folded
case-INSENSITIVE path pattern
invalid patterns are DROPPED, not fatal — the valid sibling still applies
…and the dropped pattern excludes nothing (this is the bug #2473 makes visible at the API)
no usable patterns at all -> nil matcher -> nothing excluded
root itself is never excluded
bare '**' has no slash, so it is a BASE-NAME glob — and a trailing star matches any base name
leading '/' is trimmed, so an absolute-looking pattern still matches at any depth
